text: The prostate is retracted anteriorly, positioned at the top of the screen. The bladder neck is visible behind the prostate, and the rectum is located posteriorly.
The surgeon performs an incision to develop a surgical plane just anterior to Denonvilliers' fascia, visible in the prostatectomy procedure.
Denonvier's fascia, a bilaminar structure, is visible separating the back wall of the prostate from the anterior wall of the rectum.
The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to create and develop a surgical plane in the midline, located just posterior to the prostate.
The anterior leaflet of Denonvier's fascia is visible behind the suction device in the midline, with the anterior rectal wall situated posterior to it.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to initially separate the posterior prostatic branches, followed by blunt dissection to gradually and sequentially peel them away from surrounding tissues.

The da Vinci XI robotic system is used to rotate the thirty-degree scope to look thirty degrees up, providing a clear view of the posterior surface of the prostate. This facilitates precise dissection along the prostate capsule without causing a capsulotomy.
The surgeon carefully peels away Denonvier's fascia, gradually exposing the posterior surface of the prostate using a slow and steady dissection technique.
The surgeon moves laterally, peeling away Denonvilliers' fascia to release the neurovascular bundles from the posterior side of the prostate.

The surgeon performs dissection on the patient's right side, lateralizing the tissue and gradually moving towards the midline. The dissection progresses towards the posterior aspect of the urethra, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.

The surgeon pushes back tissue and sharply divides recalcitrant strands, continuing the dissection in a caudal direction and laterally towards the curve of the prostatic contour. The neurovascular bundle on the right side is visibly released.

The surgeon returns to the midline, revealing the urethra just beyond the apex of the prostate. The dissection is anterior to Denonvier's fascia, with the urethra becoming visible as the procedure progresses.

The surgeon performs dissection on the left side, slowly and progressively peeling away Denonvilliers' fascia to preserve the intact nerve supply bilaterally, ensuring careful exposure of the prostate's posterior surface.

The posterior dissection is complete. The surgeon uses a Johan's grasper and the fourth arm, referred to as 'progress,' to retract and control the right seminal vesicle and vas deferens. This maneuver lifts the prostate anteriorly and out of the pelvis.
The surgeon creates space by manipulating the pedicle, thinning it out slightly. A haemlock clip is placed, and the dissection proceeds in an antegrade fashion towards the base of the prostate.

The surgeon applies traction to elevate the prostate, enhancing visibility of the plane between the neurovascular bundle and the base of the prostate. A clip is placed on the pedicle to secure the structure.

The surgeon uses the left hand to retract the prostate towards the midline while the right hand gently teases away the neurovascular bundle in an antegrade fashion, revisiting traction and counter-traction techniques to separate the structures.
The surgeon continues to gently tease away the neurovascular bundle from the prostate in an a-thermal fashion, ensuring careful preservation of the surrounding structures.

The surgeon carefully teases away a small capsular arterial branch from the surface of the prostate, preserving it while progressing the dissection in an antegrade fashion towards the apex. A high lateral release is performed, revealing a bulky, well-preserved neurovascular bundle on the right side.

The surgeon has successfully mobilized the neurovascular bundle down towards the apex of the prostate on the right side. The procedure then shifts to the contralateral side, where the left seminal vesicle and left vas are grasped to retract and elevate the prostate.

The surgeon visualizes and elevates the posterior aspect of the prostate using the right hand with monopolar sutures. The assistant identifies the area to place the next hemlock clip for proximal control of the vascular pedicle on the patient's left side. The pedicle is controlled in a graduated antegrade fashion, with further elevation achieved by retracting, providing direct vision of the posterior contour of the prostate.

The surgeon accurately identifies the pedicle and the start of the neurovascular bundle. A Hemolock clip is applied at the location identified by the scissors, releasing the pedicle and bringing the neurovascular bundle into clear view.

The surgeon performs a thermal dissection, revealing the previously dissected plane from the posterior dissection. The posterior work facilitates the current antegrade nerve release, making it more straightforward.

The surgeon navigates around the corner to meet the previously released area near the mid-prostatic region. A small capsular arterial branch is controlled using spot bipolar diathermy. The dissection continues with counter traction applied by the assistant.

The surgeon uses a Johan retractor to expose the basal contour of the prostate on the left side. The neurovascular bundle is protected and retracted with a bipolar grasper. Cold scissors are used to divide the anterior reflection of the lateral prostatic fascia, continuing the incision down towards the left puboprostatic ligament to preserve sufficient tissue, including the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon releases and protects the nerve while exposing the contour of the prostate apex. This preparation facilitates the upcoming dissection of the dorsal venous complex (DVC) and apical region, with much of the lateral tissue already addressed.

The surgeon approaches the dorsal venous complex (DVC), with the nerve on the left side visibly released. The anterior aspect of the prostate is secured, and attention shifts to the right side to release the anterior aspect of the lateral prostatic fascia. The neurovascular bundle is visible more laterally and posteriorly.

The prostatic contour becomes visible as the assistant uses a sucker to retract the neurovascular bundle. A large vein appears at the top of the high release of the lateral prostatic fascia, which is preserved and reflected in continuity with the neurovascular bundle. The surgeon begins to divide the dorsal venous complex (DVC) located at the twelve o'clock position in the image.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to facilitate the release of tissue, progressing towards the apex of the prostate and down to the urethra in the midline. The procedure is performed in a controlled, stepwise manner, focusing on preserving critical structures for continence and sexual function recovery, with bilateral high lateral release of the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon is nearly finished dividing the dorsal venous complex (DVC), preparing to expose the urethra. Hemostatic control is maintained with a previously placed suture through the DVC, ensuring clear visualization and preventing accidental entry into the prostate apex.

The surgeon releases the neurovascular bundle bilaterally, preserving nerve structures. The urethra is exposed, revealing an inserted catheter. The incision is made through the anterior surface of the urethra, bringing the catheter into view.

The surgeon withdraws the catheter, divides the posterior aspect of the urethra, and dissects the apex free. Both neurovascular bundles are visible and preserved, indicating a high likelihood of sexual function recovery. The catheter is reinserted through the urethra for a hemostatic check.
